china_crude: China's crude imports have halved over four weeks, plunging from 101,417 kbd (W10) to 50,334 kbd (W13), as the Hormuz closure chokes Gulf-origin supply that previously accounted for 44% of intake.

Russia has consolidated its position as China's dominant crude supplier at 14,142 kbd in W13, while Saudi Arabia and Iraq have virtually disappeared from W13 flows -- Iraq collapsed from 11,829 kbd (W10) to just 511 kbd as the FM declaration halted Basra terminal operations.
Gulf supplier share has eroded from 44.1% to 29.3% over the period, with Oman (10,789 kbd in W13) emerging as the primary Gulf-origin source thanks to its non-Hormuz export position at Mina Al-Fahal.

Pipeline flows from Russia (~600 kbd ESPO), Kazakhstan (~220 kbd), and Myanmar (~200 kbd) provide a ~1,020 kbd Hormuz-independent floor, but cannot offset the ~50,000 kbd seaborne shortfall. Watch for further drawdowns from China's pre-crisis stockpiling.
china_lng: Qatar LNG to China has gone from 362 ktons (47.6% share in W10) to zero in W13, consistent with QatarEnergy's FM extension to Chinese contracts on Mar 24.

Australia has stepped in as China's primary LNG supplier at 261 ktons in W13, supported by Malaysia (99 ktons) and Russia (64 ktons), but total imports dropped 36% from W12 to W13 indicating the market cannot fully replace lost Qatari volumes.
Gulf LNG share collapsed from 47.6% (W10) to 0% (W13) -- the fastest structural supply shift in Chinese LNG import history, driven by the complete shutdown of Qatar's 77 Mtpa capacity.
china_lpg: US LPG has surged to 52% of China's imports in W13 (235 ktons) as Middle Eastern NGL-linked supply from Saudi Arabia, UAE, and Qatar has been curtailed -- Saudi and Qatari volumes went from 64 ktons combined (W10) to zero.

Iran remains a significant supplier at 107 ktons (W13) via sanctions-circumventing ship-to-ship transfers, but total imports are volatile week-to-week (544 to 433 to 319 to 457 ktons), suggesting procurement scrambling.
The LPG-naphtha price spread is favouring increased US Gulf Coast exports to China, with PDH plant operators paying significant premiums to secure replacement cargoes.
china_kero_jet: China's jet fuel imports have ceased entirely since W12, dropping from 345 kbd (W10) to zero. South Korea was the sole supplier and appears to have redirected volumes, likely reflecting China's large domestic refining capacity and reduced aviation fuel demand amid the crisis.
china_gasoil_diesel: China's gasoil/diesel imports have stabilized at low levels (75-82 kbd in W12-W13) from a peak of 690 kbd in W10, sourced exclusively from South Korea. No Gulf exposure, reflecting China's domestic refining self-sufficiency for middle distillates.
china_gasoline: No significant gasoline imports recorded for China in W12-W13, down from 178 kbd in W10. China's massive domestic refining capacity (~18 mb/d) provides insulation from the Gulf crisis for lighter products.
china_naphtha: China's naphtha imports saw a dramatic supplier rotation: Gulf share collapsed from 76.8% (W10) to 0% (W13) as Qatar, Saudi Arabia, and UAE volumes disappeared entirely.

Russia has become the dominant naphtha supplier at 742 kbd in W13, joined by non-traditional sources Norway (536 kbd) and Thailand (332 kbd), indicating an emergency pivot by Chinese petrochemical feedstock buyers.
Total volumes dropped 31% from 2,821 kbd (W10) to 1,956 kbd (W13), squeezing petchem margins and feedstock availability.
china_sulphur: China's sulphur imports have collapsed from 94 ktons (W10, 58% from Saudi Arabia) to just 32 ktons (W13) sourced entirely from Canada, as Gulf suppliers including UAE and Qatar have been cut off by the Hormuz closure.
india_crude: India's crude imports recovered to 30,440 kbd in W13 from a trough of 25,069 kbd in W11, driven by an aggressive pivot to Russian barrels -- Russia now supplies 11,558 kbd (38% share), up from 8,263 kbd (25%) in W10.

Gulf supplier share collapsed from 44.2% (W10) to just 5.8% (W11) as the Hormuz closure hit, but has since recovered to 34.5% (W13) as Saudi Arabia (6,660 kbd) and UAE (3,400 kbd) rerouted crude via non-Hormuz pipelines (East-West Pipeline to Yanbu, ADCOP to Fujairah).
Iraq has essentially disappeared from India's supply -- from 4,574 kbd (W10) to just 427 kbd (W13), consistent with the FM on all foreign-operated oilfields and Basra terminal shutdowns.
MRPL's FM on gasoline exports and 500 kb/d refinery throughput cuts signal that supply disruption is cascading into India's downstream sector despite the sourcing shift.
india_lng: India's LNG imports bounced to 487 ktons in W13 from a low of 218 ktons in W12, with Oman emerging as the key Gulf-origin supplier at 225 ktons (46% share) -- critically, Qatar has been entirely absent since W11.
The US supplied 135 ktons in W13, confirming India's emergency procurement of American spot cargoes to replace the 41.4% of LNG imports previously sourced from Qatar's Ras Laffan.
Nigeria and Angola are providing supplementary LNG cargoes, but Gujarat Gas's 50% industrial supply cut and Petronet LNG's FM underscore that India's gas market remains severely stressed.
india_lpg: India's LPG imports have fallen 62% from 210 ktons (W10) to 80 ktons (W13), with the US now the dominant supplier at 52 ktons, as UAE and Saudi volumes that previously supplied 75% of imports have largely dried up.
Only Qatar LPG (28 ktons) remains from the Gulf in W13, down from 157 ktons combined Gulf supply in W10. The Pradhan Mantri Ujjwala cooking fuel programme faces growing supply risk.
india_kero_jet: No jet fuel imports recorded for India across the entire W10-W13 period. India's domestic refining capacity is covering aviation fuel needs, though MRPL's refinery throughput cuts may eventually create tightness.
india_gasoil_diesel: India's gasoil/diesel imports shifted from UAE (59 kbd in W10) to Russia (237 kbd W12, 110 kbd W13) as Hormuz disruption cut off Gulf product flows. Russia is filling the gap at discounted prices.
india_gasoline: Minimal gasoline imports for India (30-75 kbd), sourced exclusively from South Korea. Domestic refining capacity is largely meeting demand, though the 500 kb/d throughput cuts at MRPL are a risk factor.
india_naphtha: India's naphtha imports collapsed from 1,060 kbd (W10, 100% Gulf) to 321 kbd (W12, 100% Russia) before rebounding to 628 kbd (W13) with Oman (324 kbd) returning alongside Russia (304 kbd).

The complete disappearance of UAE, Kuwait, and Iran naphtha in W12-W13 is crippling India's petrochemical feedstock supply chain. Oman's non-Hormuz position makes it the last Gulf naphtha source standing.
india_sulphur: India's sulphur imports are sporadic -- only Qatar (33 ktons in W11) and then Canada/Jordan (74 ktons in W13) appeared across the period, reflecting the broader disruption to Gulf sulphur exports from refinery and gas processing shutdowns.
japan_crude: Japan remains the most Gulf-dependent major crude importer at 94-100% Gulf share, with Saudi Arabia and UAE supplying virtually all volumes across W10-W13.

Total imports appeared stable at ~14,700-14,950 kbd through W10-W12 before dropping to 5,754 kbd in W13 (6-day week), suggesting the SPR release of 80 mb is bridging supply but cannot sustain current demand indefinitely.
Non-Gulf diversification is minimal -- the US (1,018 kbd in W11), Malaysia, and Ecuador appeared briefly but Gulf dependence remains structurally above 85%.
Qatar crude re-emerged at small volumes (637 kbd W12, 224 kbd W13), likely from pre-loaded cargoes rather than new production given the total field shutdown.
japan_lng: Japan's LNG imports are trending down from 1,305 ktons (W10) to 840 ktons (W13), with Australia consistently dominant (516 ktons in W13, 61% share) as the nuclear restart provides partial demand relief.
Qatar LNG appeared at small volumes in W11-W12 (75-77 ktons) but was absent in W10 and W13, confirming the FM has effectively eliminated Japanese access to Qatari supply.
Japan has diversified to Canada (141 ktons W12), Indonesia (204 ktons W11), and Papua New Guinea (70-135 ktons), but no single source can replace Qatar's pre-crisis contribution.
japan_lpg: Japan's LPG imports are now 100% US-sourced (208 ktons in W13), a complete shift from the pre-crisis mix that included Saudi Arabia and Qatar. The US Gulf Coast has replaced the entire Middle Eastern LPG supply chain for Japan.
japan_kero_jet: Japan's jet fuel imports are sourced entirely from South Korea (490 kbd in W13), steady across the period. No Gulf dependency for this product.
japan_gasoil_diesel: Sporadic gasoil/diesel imports for Japan -- 0 in W10, 115 kbd (S. Korea) in W11, 369 kbd (mainly Brunei) in W12, then back to 0 in W13. Domestic refining is covering most demand.
japan_gasoline: Japan's gasoline imports from South Korea have risen sharply from 99 kbd (W10) to 598 kbd (W13), suggesting domestic refinery throughput cuts are beginning to impact lighter product availability.
japan_naphtha: Japan's naphtha imports remain heavily Gulf-dependent but volatile: 1,510 kbd (W10, 100% Gulf) to 2,021 kbd (W11, 41% Gulf with new US/Peru/India sources) to 605 kbd (W12, 100% Qatar) to 1,322 kbd (W13, 71% UAE).
The UAE emerged as the dominant naphtha supplier in W13 at 935 kbd, likely via the ADCOP Fujairah pipeline. Japan's petrochemical sector is scrambling to maintain feedstock flows.
japan_sulphur: No sulphur imports recorded for Japan across the entire W10-W13 period.
south_korea_crude: South Korea's crude imports have declined 62% from 23,961 kbd (W10) to 9,206 kbd (W13), the most severe contraction among major Asian importers.
The US became the top non-Gulf supplier at 5,645 kbd in W12, but Gulf-origin crude still accounted for 72% of W13 imports (UAE at 4,120 kbd, Saudi Arabia at 1,528 kbd, Iraq at 951 kbd).
The 22.5 mb SPR release (5 mb already delivered by Mar 25) is providing critical bridging supply while YNCC's petrochemical FM signals downstream stress from reduced refinery throughput.
south_korea_lng: South Korea's LNG supply has diversified well despite the crisis: Australia leads (335 ktons W13), supplemented by Indonesia (119 ktons), Malaysia (68 ktons), US, and China re-exports.
Qatar's contribution dropped from 75 ktons (W10) to zero in W13, but KOGAS's new 10-year 3.3 mtpa Trafigura deal for US LNG demonstrates proactive supply chain resilience.
Gulf share has fallen from 7.9% (already low) to 0% in W13 -- South Korea's early diversification strategy is paying off.
south_korea_lpg: South Korea's LPG imports are entirely from US and Canada (47 ktons in W13), with zero Gulf exposure across all four weeks. The pre-crisis shift to US Gulf Coast supply has fully insulated Korea's LPG market.
south_korea_kero_jet: Minimal jet fuel imports for South Korea -- only 89 kbd from China in W11, zero in other weeks. Domestic refining capacity is sufficient.
south_korea_gasoil_diesel: South Korea's gasoil/diesel imports are small and non-Gulf (0-436 kbd), sourced from Japan, China, and Russia. Domestic mega-refineries (SK, S-Oil, GS Caltex, Hyundai) are self-sufficient for middle distillates.
south_korea_gasoline: South Korea's gasoline imports shifted from Japan (535-756 kbd in W10-W11) to Singapore (167 kbd W12) to zero in W13, suggesting inventory drawdowns or domestic production adjustment.
south_korea_naphtha: South Korea's naphtha imports declined from 4,655 kbd (W10) to 2,408 kbd (W13), with Gulf share dropping from 56% to 30% as Kuwait, UAE, and Qatar volumes fell.

The supplier base has fragmented dramatically -- from 13 countries in W10 to 8 in W13, with non-traditional sources like Greece (436 kbd), Peru (345 kbd), and Libya (186 kbd) appearing as petchem feedstock procurement becomes increasingly opportunistic.
south_korea_sulphur: No sulphur imports recorded for South Korea across the W10-W13 period.
thailand_crude: Thailand's crude imports have narrowed dramatically from 7 suppliers (W10) to just 2 in W13 (UAE at 4,905 kbd and Malaysia at 357 kbd), with UAE now supplying 93% of crude via the ADCOP-Fujairah route.
The shift to near-total UAE dependence (up from 33% in W10) creates concentration risk if Fujairah port operations are further disrupted by drone attacks.
US crude, which surged to 3,140 kbd in W11 as an emergency bridge, has completely disappeared from W12-W13 as logistics settle into a more concentrated non-Hormuz supply pattern.
thailand_lng: Thailand's LNG imports dropped from 367 ktons (W10, 52% from Qatar) to 213 ktons (W13, zero Qatar), with Australia (76 ktons), Oman (70 ktons), and China re-exports (68 ktons) filling the gap.
The diversification to Oman LNG in W13 is a new development -- Thailand's declining Gulf of Thailand domestic gas production makes LNG security increasingly critical.
thailand_lpg: Negligible LPG imports for Thailand (0-7 ktons) across the period. Domestic production and relatively low Middle East LPG dependence provide insulation.
thailand_kero_jet: No jet fuel imports recorded for Thailand in W10-W13.
thailand_gasoil_diesel: Small, variable gasoil/diesel imports for Thailand (38-319 kbd) from non-Gulf sources including China, Malaysia, and India. No direct Gulf exposure.
thailand_gasoline: Thailand's gasoline imports from Singapore dropped from 50 kbd (W10) to zero (W13), indicating domestic refining capacity adjustment or demand contraction.
thailand_naphtha: Thailand's naphtha imports collapsed from 796 kbd (W10, 63% from Bahrain) to near-zero (22 kbd from Singapore in W13), as Bapco's Sitra refinery destruction eliminated Bahrain's naphtha exports entirely after W11.
thailand_sulphur: Thailand received only one sulphur cargo in the period -- 19 ktons from Russia in W10. Gulf sulphur supply disruption is evident.
vietnam_crude: Vietnam's extreme Kuwait dependence (100% of crude in W10-W11 at 2,000 kbd) has been fully severed -- W12 brought only 618 kbd from Nigeria, and W13 saw zero crude imports.
The government's emergency cut of MFN oil import tariffs to 0% reflects the severity of the supply crisis for Nghi Son Refinery, which was designed around Kuwaiti crude grades.
vietnam_lng: Vietnam received one small Qatar LNG cargo (62 ktons in W11) before the FM cut supply. No LNG imports in W10, W12, or W13. The Thi Vai terminal is likely running on the February cargo with limited buffer.
vietnam_lpg: Vietnam's LPG imports surged from 3 ktons (W10-W11, China) to 71 ktons (W13, US) as American cargoes replaced negligible Middle Eastern supply. The shift to US sourcing is a direct consequence of Gulf disruption.
vietnam_kero_jet: Vietnam's jet fuel imports are declining (232 to 142 to 66 to 93 kbd) and sourced from regional suppliers (Thailand, China, Singapore). No Gulf exposure.
vietnam_gasoil_diesel: Vietnam's gasoil/diesel imports surged from 672 kbd (W10) to 1,007 kbd (W13), sourced almost entirely from South Korea. This likely reflects domestic refining shortfalls as crude supply to Nghi Son has been disrupted.
vietnam_gasoline: Vietnam's gasoline imports are declining (442 to 303 to 119 to 82 kbd), sourced from Malaysia, Singapore, and Japan. Falling volumes may indicate demand destruction from rising fuel prices.
vietnam_naphtha: No naphtha imports recorded for Vietnam across W10-W13.
vietnam_sulphur: Vietnam's sulphur imports shifted from Qatar (17 ktons W10) to Jordan (19 ktons W12), then ceased. Gulf sulphur disruption is forcing procurement rotation.
eu_27_crude: EU-27 crude exports (intra-EU and to neighbours) fell from 3,047 kbd (W10) to 324 kbd (W12) before recovering to 1,006 kbd (W13), as European refineries adjust throughput amid rising input costs from the Gulf crisis.
Finland remains a consistent destination (599 kbd) for likely North Sea/Baltic crude re-exports, while Brazil (2,132 kbd in W10) has disappeared from later weeks.
eu_27_lng: EU-27 LNG exports are negligible (0-13 ktons), reflecting Europe's position as a net LNG importer. The single 13-kton Italy-bound cargo in W10 was likely a re-export.
eu_27_lpg: Small intra-European LPG trade (12-32 ktons), primarily to Poland (dominant) and Ireland. No Gulf relevance -- this is domestic redistribution.
eu_27_kero_jet: EU-27 jet fuel exports dropped sharply from 1,025 kbd (W11) to 69 kbd (W13), as European refineries facing higher crude costs reduce output of aviation fuel for export.
eu_27_gasoil_diesel: EU-27 gasoil/diesel exports declined from 7,696 kbd (W11) to 2,582 kbd (W13), with the UK, Poland, and intra-EU destinations dominating. The contraction reflects tightening European diesel supply amid Gulf crude disruption.
The shift suggests European refineries are prioritising domestic diesel supply over exports as input costs surge.
eu_27_gasoline: EU-27 gasoline exports remain robust at 6,867 kbd (W13), increasingly directed to Africa (Morocco, Senegal, Ghana, Tunisia, Mauritania) as traditional Gulf-supplied markets in Asia redirect purchases to European surplus.
The shift toward West African destinations in W12-W13 suggests Gulf gasoline disruption is creating secondary trade flow redirections.
eu_27_naphtha: EU-27 naphtha exports are variable (1,409-2,959 kbd), with Asian destinations (China, Japan, Taiwan) appearing in W11-W13 as Gulf naphtha supply disruption creates arbitrage opportunities for European refiners.
eu_27_sulphur: No sulphur exports recorded for EU-27 across W10-W13.
united_states_crude: US crude imports are steady at 11,794-21,930 kbd, with Saudi Arabia consistently in the top 3 suppliers (2,410-4,121 kbd), confirming that Yanbu/Red Sea route shipments are reaching US refineries.
Venezuela (2,319-3,452 kbd) and Mexico (1,553-3,943 kbd) remain critical Western Hemisphere suppliers, while Gulf share has drifted from 27.5% (W10) to 18.1% (W12) then 27.8% (W13).
The 172 mb SPR release (43% of the IEA's record 400 mb coordinated release) with first deliveries from Mar 20 is supplementing commercial imports.
united_states_lng: No LNG imports for the US across W10-W13. As the world's largest LNG exporter, the US is a net supplier drawing maximum utilisation from Sabine Pass, Freeport, and Plaquemines facilities.
united_states_lpg: Negligible US LPG imports (0-12 ktons). The US is a major net LPG exporter benefiting from Gulf supply disruption.
united_states_kero_jet: US jet fuel imports surged from 91 kbd (W10) to 1,428 kbd (W11) then stabilised at 518-611 kbd, sourced primarily from South Korea and Canada. This reflects US refinery product mix adjustments during the crisis.
united_states_gasoil_diesel: US gasoil/diesel imports are modest (533-1,694 kbd) and declining, sourced from Canada (dominant) and Turkey. No Gulf exposure -- the US is self-sufficient in middle distillates.
united_states_gasoline: US gasoline imports remain steady at 1,254-2,598 kbd from Canada, Bahamas, UK, and Caribbean sources. No Gulf dependence, though European gasoline is appearing (UK at 85-405 kbd) as trade flows adjust.
united_states_naphtha: US naphtha imports are shifting from European sources (Belgium, France, Netherlands in W10-W12) to Latin American/African sources (Angola 345 kbd, Argentina 106 kbd, Brazil 101 kbd in W13) as global naphtha trade reroutes.
united_states_sulphur: No sulphur imports for the US across W10-W13.
taiwan_crude: Taiwan's crude imports are undergoing a rapid de-Gulfification: Gulf share fell from 99.3% (W10, almost entirely Saudi Arabia at 2,600 kbd) to 0% (W13, 100% US at 2,061 kbd).
The US has become Taiwan's sole crude supplier in W13, a dramatic strategic shift from near-total Saudi/UAE dependence just three weeks prior.
Kuwait appeared in W11 (2,000 kbd) for likely pre-loaded cargoes but has since disappeared. Taiwan's small SPR provides limited buffer if US supply is disrupted.
taiwan_lng: Taiwan's LNG supply is diversifying successfully: Qatar share fell from 18% (W10) to 0% (W13), replaced by increased Australian volumes (290 ktons, 58% of W13 total).
Oman LNG (70 ktons) provides residual Gulf-origin supply, while Brunei (69 ktons) and Equatorial Guinea (73 ktons) have emerged as new sources reflecting Taiwan's aggressive spot procurement.
taiwan_lpg: Taiwan's LPG imports have dried up -- from 17 ktons (W10, Australia) to zero in W12-W13, reflecting broader Asian LPG market tightening.
taiwan_kero_jet: No jet fuel imports recorded for Taiwan across W10-W13.
taiwan_gasoil_diesel: Taiwan's gasoil/diesel imports shifted from South Korea (675 kbd W11) to China/Russia mix (254 kbd W12, 211 kbd W13), indicating a broadening of product supply sources during the crisis.
taiwan_gasoline: Minimal gasoline imports for Taiwan -- only 176 kbd from Japan in W12, zero in other weeks.
taiwan_naphtha: Taiwan's naphtha imports pivoted from zero Gulf (W10, Singapore/US) to 100% Gulf (W11-W12, UAE at 664-892 kbd) then back to zero in W13, suggesting UAE naphtha via Fujairah was temporarily available before being redirected.
taiwan_sulphur: No sulphur imports recorded for Taiwan across W10-W13.
bahrain_crude: No crude exports from Bahrain across W10-W13. The Sitra refinery (405 kb/d) was struck by Iranian drones on Mar 4 and Mar 9, and Bapco Energies declared FM on all operations -- the facility is offline indefinitely.
bahrain_lng: No LNG exports from Bahrain. The country is an LNG importer dependent on Saudi pipeline gas, and faces acute energy security risk from the regional supply chain disruption.
bahrain_lpg: No LPG exports from Bahrain across W10-W13, consistent with the complete Bapco refinery shutdown and FM declaration.
bahrain_kero_jet: Bahrain exported 503 kbd of jet fuel to UAE in W10 only, then zero for W11-W13. The Sitra refinery destruction has eliminated all product exports.
bahrain_gasoil_diesel: No gasoil/diesel exports from Bahrain across W10-W13 following the Sitra refinery shutdown.
bahrain_gasoline: No gasoline exports from Bahrain across W10-W13.
bahrain_naphtha: Bahrain exported 512 kbd of naphtha to Japan in W10, then zero for W11-W13. This was likely the last pre-strike cargo before the Sitra refinery was destroyed.
bahrain_sulphur: No sulphur exports from Bahrain across W10-W13.
iran_crude: Iran's crude exports have declined from 12,920 kbd (W10) to 8,000 kbd (W13), a 38% drop despite Iran being the conflict aggressor -- USAF strikes on Kharg Island military targets (Mar 13) and Israeli strikes on South Pars are constraining output.
China was the only identified destination (4,604 kbd in W10) but destination data is opaque in W11-W13, consistent with shadow-fleet tanker operations and ship-to-ship transfers to evade tracking.
The decline from 14,083 kbd (W11 peak) to 8,000 kbd suggests the military strikes on South Pars gas infrastructure (~12% of Iran's gas offline) are having a cascading effect on oil-associated output.
iran_lng: No LNG exports from Iran. Despite holding the world's second-largest gas reserves, Iran has no LNG export infrastructure.
iran_lpg: Iran's LPG exports collapsed from 63 ktons (W10) to near-zero visible volumes in W11-W13 (23-41 ktons, destinations undisclosed), reflecting both Hormuz disruption and the ~30% shut-in of southern NGL-linked oil fields.
iran_kero_jet: No jet fuel exports from Iran across W10-W13.
iran_gasoil_diesel: No gasoil/diesel exports from Iran across W10-W13.
iran_gasoline: No gasoline exports from Iran across W10-W13.
iran_naphtha: Iran's naphtha exports went from 89 kbd (W10) to 320 kbd (W11, of which 211 kbd to UAE) then zero in W12-W13. The brief W11 UAE flow may reflect pre-conflict vessel movements.
iran_sulphur: No sulphur exports from Iran across W10-W13.
iraq_crude: Iraq's crude exports have collapsed 90% from 15,283 kbd (W10) to 1,500 kbd (W12-W13), with the only remaining exports flowing via the Kirkuk-Ceyhan pipeline to Turkey after the FM declaration on all foreign-operated oilfields (Mar 20).
Basra output has fallen to ~800 kb/d from 3.3 mb/d as storage fills and southern terminal operations have halted entirely. BP was ordered to cut North Rumaila to 350 kb/d.

The Kirkuk-Ceyhan pipeline is now Iraq's only functioning export route, making Turkey the sole destination. China, India, and Italy -- previously the top three destinations -- have received zero Iraqi crude since W11.
iraq_lng: No LNG exports from Iraq. The country has no LNG export infrastructure, and Basra Gas Company flaring-to-capture projects are stalled alongside the crude output collapse.
iraq_lpg: Iraq's LPG exports effectively ceased after W10 (11 ktons), consistent with the Basra terminal shutdown and FM declaration on all operations.
iraq_kero_jet: No jet fuel exports from Iraq across W10-W13.
iraq_gasoil_diesel: No gasoil/diesel exports from Iraq across W10-W13.
iraq_gasoline: No gasoline exports from Iraq across W10-W13.
iraq_naphtha: Small, intermittent naphtha exports from Iraq (104 kbd W11, 91 kbd W13) with undisclosed destinations, likely residual volumes from northern production routed via Turkey.
iraq_sulphur: No sulphur exports from Iraq across W10-W13.
kuwait_crude: Kuwait's crude exports collapsed from 8,135 kbd (W10) to zero (W11, W13) with only a tiny 546 kbd in W12, after KPC declared FM on crude exports and the Al-Ahmadi refinery was struck by Iranian drones on Mar 19-20.
China was the only identified destination in W10 (2,000 kbd). The Hormuz closure and refinery destruction have effectively shut down Kuwait's entire petroleum export apparatus.
kuwait_lng: No LNG exports from Kuwait. Kuwait is an LNG importer via the Al-Zour FSRU, and faces acute domestic power generation risk as Qatar's LNG is entirely offline.
kuwait_lpg: Kuwait's LPG exports ceased after W10 (23 ktons), consistent with the refinery damage and export infrastructure shutdown.
kuwait_kero_jet: Kuwait exported 64 kbd of jet fuel (to UAE) in W10 only, then zero for W11-W13 following the Al-Ahmadi refinery strikes.
kuwait_gasoil_diesel: Kuwait's gasoil/diesel exports fell from 941 kbd (W10) to 714 kbd (W11) then zero in W12-W13, as the Al-Ahmadi refinery went offline after drone strikes and throughput cuts cascaded.
kuwait_gasoline: Kuwait exported 448 kbd of gasoline in W11 only (likely pre-strike inventory), then zero in W12-W13.
kuwait_naphtha: No naphtha exports from Kuwait across W10-W13.
kuwait_sulphur: Minimal sulphur exports from Kuwait (18 ktons in W11-W12), then zero in W13.
oman_crude: Oman's crude exports have recovered to 7,632 kbd (W13) from a trough of 3,850 kbd (W11), nearly matching pre-crisis levels of 7,800 kbd (W10) -- a standout recovery driven by Oman's non-Hormuz export position at Mina Al-Fahal.

China absorbs virtually all Oman crude (7,632 kbd in W13), with Taiwan appearing in W12 (1,885 kbd), confirming Oman's strategic importance as the last major Gulf crude exporter with unimpeded sea access.
Oman's inland production base was largely unaffected by the conflict despite the Iranian drone strike on Salalah port oil tanks (Mar 11), giving it a unique competitive advantage.
oman_lng: Oman LNG's 10.4 Mtpa facility is one of the few Middle Eastern LNG operations still running, exporting 148-303 ktons weekly to India, Japan, South Korea, Taiwan, and Thailand.
With Qatar's 77 Mtpa offline, Oman LNG cargoes are commanding significant Asian spot premiums. India (77-154 ktons) and Japan (68-77 ktons) are the primary customers.
oman_lpg: No LPG exports from Oman recorded across W10-W13.
oman_kero_jet: Sporadic jet fuel exports from Oman (58 kbd W10, 172 kbd W12), then zero in W11 and W13.
oman_gasoil_diesel: Oman's gasoil/diesel exports remain active at 682 kbd (W13), down from 1,005 kbd (W10) but consistent. The non-Hormuz position allows continued product exports while Gulf neighbours are shut in.
oman_gasoline: Oman's gasoline exports surged to 843 kbd in W13 from 300 kbd (W11), potentially filling regional demand as Kuwait, Bahrain, and UAE gasoline exports have ceased. Oman's operational refineries are running at high utilisation.
oman_naphtha: Oman's naphtha exports dropped from 813 kbd (W10) to zero in W12-W13. This may reflect feedstock prioritisation for domestic petrochemical use as regional supply tightens.
oman_sulphur: No sulphur exports from Oman across W10-W13.
qatar_crude: Qatar's crude exports collapsed from 2,047 kbd (W10) to effectively zero, with only a small 822 kbd cargo to UAE in W12. All offshore oil fields (~440 kb/d including Al Shaheen) have been shut since early March.
QatarEnergy's FM encompasses all hydrocarbon exports. The 12.8 Mtpa of LNG capacity damaged by Mar 18 missile strikes will take 3-5 years to repair, and crude cannot restart until the conflict ends.
qatar_lng: Qatar's LNG exports have collapsed from 370 ktons (W10) to near-zero, with only tiny residual volumes in W12-W13 (46-129 ktons to Kuwait). This represents the removal of ~20% of global LNG supply (~7M tonnes/month).
The FM was extended to Italy, Belgium, South Korea, and China on Mar 24. Asian spot LNG surged ~39% and European TTF spiked 52% within 24 hours of the initial shutdown.

QatarEnergy CEO confirmed restart is impossible until the conflict ends, plus a minimum 2-week restart for cryogenic equipment. The 12.8 Mtpa of infrastructure damaged on Mar 18 is offline for 3-5 years.
qatar_lpg: Qatar's LPG exports ceased after W10 (32 ktons, 23 to India), consistent with the complete halt of all downstream operations at Ras Laffan since Mar 2.
qatar_kero_jet: No jet fuel exports from Qatar across W10-W13.
qatar_gasoil_diesel: No gasoil/diesel exports from Qatar across W10-W13. All refining capacity (Laffan 1+2, Mesaieed) is offline.
qatar_gasoline: No gasoline exports from Qatar across W10-W13.
qatar_naphtha: Qatar's naphtha exports went from 116 kbd (W10) to zero for W11-W13, as all Ras Laffan condensate processing has been halted.
qatar_sulphur: Qatar's sulphur exports dropped from 72 ktons (W10) to zero in W11 and W13 with a small 37-kton cargo in W12, reflecting the shutdown of gas processing that produces sulphur as a byproduct.
russia_crude: Russia's crude exports surged 41% from 33,451 kbd (W10) to 47,314 kbd (W11), capitalising on the Gulf supply vacuum. China (18,027 kbd) and India (10,021 kbd) dominated as refiners pivoted to discounted Urals and ESPO barrels.

Exports have since moderated to 28,658 kbd (W13), but European flows remain surprisingly strong -- Netherlands (1,767 kbd), France (1,078 kbd), Italy (1,059 kbd) continue importing despite sanctions, with 68% of Russian crude reportedly moving on shadow-fleet tankers.

The ESPO pipeline provides ~600 kbd of Hormuz-independent supply to China. Russia is the clearest strategic beneficiary of the Gulf crisis, with China absorbing 52% of Russian fossil fuel export revenue.
russia_lng: Russia's LNG exports are steady at 485-779 ktons weekly, with Yamal and Arctic LNG-2 positioned to capture marginal Asian demand as Qatar's 77 Mtpa shutdown creates the largest LNG supply deficit in history.
Key markets are Spain (74-220 ktons), Belgium (73-147 ktons), France (74-221 ktons), and Japan (64-135 ktons). Asian spot demand for Russian cargoes is surging.
russia_lpg: Minimal LPG exports from Russia (0-21 ktons), exclusively to Turkey when active. Russia's LPG share remains a small part of global trade.
russia_kero_jet: No jet fuel exports from Russia across W10-W13.
russia_gasoil_diesel: Russia's gasoil/diesel exports fell sharply from 6,525 kbd (W11) to 1,675 kbd (W13). Despite crude exports surging, product exports are declining -- likely reflecting domestic fuel prioritisation or refinery constraints.
russia_gasoline: Russia's gasoline exports dropped to zero in W12-W13 from 263-269 kbd in W10-W11, consistent with Russia's seasonal domestic fuel priority and potential refinery maintenance.
russia_naphtha: Russia's naphtha exports declined from 4,420 kbd (W11) to 1,514 kbd (W13), after initially surging to fill the Gulf naphtha gap. China is the primary beneficiary, with Russian naphtha replacing Qatari and Saudi volumes.
russia_sulphur: Russia's sulphur exports are small but present (31-78 ktons), providing an alternative to Gulf sulphur that has gone offline with gas processing shutdowns across Qatar, UAE, and Saudi Arabia.
saudi_arabia_crude: Saudi Arabia's crude exports dropped 39% from 44,659 kbd (W10) to 27,115 kbd (W13) as four major offshore fields remain shut since Mar 9, but the rerouting of crude via the East-West Pipeline to Yanbu has been the critical lifeline.
Yanbu loadings have surged 220% to ~2.2 mb/d, and the destination mix has concentrated dramatically around China (7,706 kbd), India (7,201 kbd), and Myanmar (2,010 kbd).
Egypt volumes dropped from 16,191 kbd (W10) to zero in W13, while South Korea and Japan have disappeared from Saudi supply -- these Hormuz-dependent routes are fully blocked.
The pipeline bypass strategy is working but cannot offset the ~20-25% production cut from offshore field closures. Watch for further Yanbu capacity constraints.
saudi_arabia_lng: No LNG exports from Saudi Arabia. The kingdom has no LNG export capacity -- the Jafurah unconventional gas development is in early construction. The conflict has underscored the strategic urgency to diversify beyond crude oil.
saudi_arabia_lpg: Saudi LPG exports have been curtailed to just 23 ktons (W12-W13, to India/Jordan) from zero in W10, reflecting reduced NGL throughput from offshore field closures and the Mar 2-13 Ras Tanura shutdown.
saudi_arabia_kero_jet: Saudi jet fuel exports dropped from 698 kbd (W10) to zero (W13), as the offshore field shutdowns reduce condensate feedstock for refinery kero/jet production.
saudi_arabia_gasoil_diesel: Saudi gasoil/diesel exports declined 32% from 5,201 kbd (W10) to 3,559 kbd (W13), still substantial -- suggesting Yanbu-accessible refinery capacity (Yanbu refinery, SAMREF) is maintaining product output for export.
saudi_arabia_gasoline: Saudi gasoline exports have been volatile (1,565 to 963 to 1,924 to 613 kbd), trending down in W13 as the refinery throughput cuts from offshore field closures cascade into lighter product availability.
saudi_arabia_naphtha: Saudi naphtha exports surged from near-zero (94 kbd W10) to 837 kbd (W11) then moderated to 510 kbd (W13), likely reflecting Yanbu refinery condensate processing -- a product that was not historically a major Saudi export.
saudi_arabia_sulphur: Saudi sulphur exports declined from 86 ktons (W11) to zero (W13) as gas processing throughput has been reduced alongside crude production cuts.
uae_crude: UAE crude exports fell from 22,343 kbd (W10) to a trough of 9,265 kbd (W12) before rebounding to 19,227 kbd (W13), driven by ADNOC's rerouting of residual exports via the 1 mb/d ADCOP pipeline to Fujairah.
India (5,595 kbd), South Korea (3,850 kbd), and Japan (1,900 kbd) were the key W13 destinations, with the Philippines (1,000 kbd) emerging as a new buyer.
The W12 trough coincided with reported drone attacks on Fujairah port disrupting loading operations. The W13 recovery suggests port operations have partially stabilised.
UAE output has fallen by more than half from pre-crisis levels, but the ADCOP pipeline makes the UAE one of only two Gulf producers (alongside Saudi Arabia) maintaining significant exports.
uae_lng: UAE LNG exports from Das Island have collapsed from 63 ktons (W10, to India) to zero in W11-W13, as the Hormuz blockage forces operations to minimal capacity and the Habshan gas complex resumed only on Mar 23 after a 4-day emergency shutdown.
uae_lpg: UAE LPG exports declined from 55 ktons (W10) to zero in W12-W13, as the Shah Gas Field remains shut and Ruwais Refinery-2 (a major NGL source) is offline after drone strikes.
uae_kero_jet: No jet fuel exports from UAE across W10-W13.
uae_gasoil_diesel: UAE gasoil/diesel exports fell from 280-511 kbd (W10-W11) to zero in W12-W13, as refinery throughput has been sharply curtailed.
uae_gasoline: UAE gasoline exports went from 982 kbd (W10) to zero for W11-W13, reflecting the severe downstream impact of the Hormuz closure and facility damage on UAE's refining complex.
uae_naphtha: UAE naphtha exports show an unusual pattern: 557 kbd (W10), zero (W11-W12), then 323 kbd (W13). The W13 recovery suggests Fujairah-accessible condensate processing has partially resumed.
uae_sulphur: No sulphur exports from UAE across W10-W13.
us_crude: US crude exports are running at maximum capacity at 25,556-27,503 kbd, making the US the primary non-Gulf crude supplier globally. The 172 mb SPR release (43% of IEA's 400 mb coordinated release) supplements commercial exports.
Key destinations include Netherlands (2,113-4,920 kbd), South Korea (810-5,647 kbd), UK (1,400-2,607 kbd), and India (1,207-1,741 kbd), with Taiwan and Thailand also pulling significant US barrels.
The breadth of destinations (15+ countries per week) reflects the US's role as the swing supplier backstopping the global crude market during the worst Gulf disruption in history.
us_lng: US LNG exports are the world's largest at 2,204-2,600 ktons weekly, providing the primary offset to Qatar's total shutdown. Sabine Pass, Freeport, and Plaquemines are running at maximum utilisation.

The customer base spans 15-20 countries per week, with Europe (Germany, France, Spain, Netherlands, Belgium) and Asia (Japan, South Korea, India, Thailand, Egypt, Turkey) competing for limited spot cargoes.
The ~20% global LNG supply gap from Qatar has driven Asian spot prices up ~39% and European gas up ~50%, creating enormous revenue upside for US LNG producers.
us_lpg: US LPG exports are robust at 893-1,042 ktons weekly, distributed across 15-20 countries. Japan (68-237 ktons), Indonesia (23-80 ktons), and Mexico (48-121 ktons) are consistent buyers.
The US Gulf Coast has effectively replaced Middle Eastern NGL-linked LPG supply for most Asian importers, with the LPG-naphtha spread favouring increased US exports.
us_kero_jet: US jet fuel exports surged from 1,074 kbd (W10) to 2,424 kbd (W12) as the US fills global aviation fuel gaps. South Korea is a major destination alongside the usual Latin American and European buyers.
us_gasoil_diesel: US gasoil/diesel exports are running at elevated levels of 6,360-8,405 kbd, with broad distribution to Latin America, Europe, and Asia. The US has become the global swing diesel supplier during the crisis.
us_gasoline: US gasoline exports remain strong at 4,952-6,004 kbd, directed primarily to Latin America and West Africa. Demand pull from Africa has increased as Gulf gasoline supply (Kuwait, UAE, Saudi Arabia) has been curtailed.
us_naphtha: US naphtha exports surged to 5,169 kbd in W12 before moderating to 2,631 kbd in W13. Asian and Latin American petrochemical sectors are increasingly dependent on US feedstock as Gulf naphtha dries up.
us_sulphur: Minimal US sulphur exports (0-44 ktons), suggesting the global sulphur market is tightening as Gulf gas processing shutdowns reduce output.
australia_crude: Australia's crude exports are modest at 1,277-2,178 kbd, directed to regional refineries in South Korea, Thailand, Indonesia, Singapore, and Brunei. Not a significant global swing supplier.
australia_lng: Australia has become the single most critical LNG supplier globally, with exports at 940-1,760 ktons weekly flowing to Japan (339-583 ktons), China (143-404 ktons), South Korea (104-343 ktons), and Taiwan (73-371 ktons).

With Qatar's 77 Mtpa offline, Australian LNG cargoes are commanding significant premiums. Exports surged 26% from W10 to W11 as operators maximised output, but have since tapered to 940 ktons in W13, suggesting facilities are near capacity limits.
Australia's LNG is now the single largest offset to the Qatar shutdown, and any operational disruption at NW Shelf, Gorgon, or Wheatstone would have immediate global price impact.
australia_lpg: Australia's LPG exports are negligible (0-45 ktons) and directed to China when active.
australia_kero_jet: No jet fuel exports from Australia across W10-W13.
australia_gasoil_diesel: No gasoil/diesel exports from Australia across W10-W13.
australia_gasoline: No gasoline exports from Australia across W10-W13.
australia_naphtha: Small, intermittent naphtha exports from Australia (345 kbd in W11 and W13), reflecting limited condensate-based output.
australia_sulphur: No sulphur exports from Australia across W10-W13.
